result:
# The Catalog of Unfinished Things

There are days when the world feels like a museum of incomplete sentences. You walk past buildings wrapped in mesh, scaffolds that look like ladders leading nowhere, a construction crane standing idle while workers take a break and drink something warm. The pavement stops mid-block and gives way to a tangle of dirt and weeds, a fence with a cheerful sign that says “Under Construction,” which is less cheerful when you read it again. A man is trying to fix a leaky faucet in his apartment. A woman is learning to speak Spanish again after years of silence. Two neighbors are arguing over a sidewalk, half of it already poured, the other half waiting for a decision. Your phone reminds you of five unread texts, the last one from three weeks ago. The kitchen counter has a stack of receipts you’ll file when you have time, and a jar of flour is open, catching dust.

The unfinished can be a feeling and a structure and a way of living. It lives in the corners where we don’t always look and in the center where we pretend we don’t see. It lives in the places we keep meaning to finish, but then we decide that finishing it would ruin it, because finishing would mean stopping. And stopping is often a kind of loss.

I love the unfinished for a thousand reasons that don’t always align. I love it for the promise it keeps—the sense that there is still a possibility here, a future you can walk toward. I love it for its honesty, the way it shows the process without hiding it. I love it for how it makes us talk, because the unfinished cannot stand on its own. It leans on us.

Let’s begin with the city. I mean this literally, not as a metaphor, because the literal often grounds the metaphor. There is a block downtown where the old warehouse turned into something like a market, but the new brick has not yet met the old bricks, and the windows are covered with a black mesh to keep birds from nesting inside during construction, which has not yet resumed. People sit at tables with laptops, sipping coffee from paper cups, pretending to read the news while actually scrolling through photos of their friends. A little girl is on her scooter, making circles that are too tight for an adult’s patience but perfect for her own sense of joy. A sign says “Soft Opening Today,” which, to the child, is as good as a celebration. To the parent, it means a place where half the stalls sell nothing but air.

Walking into such a place is like walking into a conversation that has paused mid-sentence. You get used to the rhythm, the way that pauses matter because they are not empty. A pause carries meaning: a breath before a difficult question, a smile hiding a worry, a look that says, “I think we both want the same thing.” The city builds itself in these pauses, in the long quiet periods between permits and deliveries, the weather that changes plans, the budget that changes minds.

There is something generous about unfinished work. It invites us. When you pass a mural that is almost done, you want to see the missing part. When you see a website with a single button that doesn’t work, you can help by clicking it anyway. When you see a bookshelf with half its shelves cut from wood that still smells like the forest, you imagine the books you might place there and wonder if they will fit. The unfinished makes you an actor in someone else’s story. The finished asks you to sit down and watch.

This is a small example, but it is one that makes me think. A friend once told me about a piece of software he wrote to manage his personal finances. He had a spreadsheet in Excel that he had used for years. He started building an app to replace it. He wrote the login page. He made a button that changed color when you hovered over it, and he was proud of it. Then the app sat there for six months. He would open it every few days, look at the button, and close it. He told me he was waiting for a breakthrough in the design. I asked him whether waiting would work better if he made a prototype, something he could touch. He said yes, but he didn’t do it. Then, one day, he got a new job and moved and left the app behind, partly because he realized that the spreadsheet had grown too, in his mind, and that the app was an attempt to solve something he had not fully named. The app’s unfinishedness became a kind of completion: it was finished as a pause. He had named his need by not finishing.

The unfinished gives structure to our lives. Without it, our days would be like a list of checkboxes without questions. A checklist is comforting and also boring if there are no open lines for you to write what actually matters. The unfinished is a form. It invites us to answer. We fill the empty space. We say what we meant when we said that we would paint the living room, that we would try the new restaurant, that we would call our cousin, that we would learn French. The emptiness of the unfinished is like the emptiness of a poem before it is written. It is the place where you will place your own breath.

I do not mean to romanticize all unfinished work. There is the unfinished that is carelessness, the unfinished that is denial, the unfinished that is a promise you made because you were afraid to say no. There are unfinished dishes piled in the sink, unfinished repairs that never quite become finished, unfinished arguments that get worse with silence. There are political commitments that become stale because they never become action. There are software features that become so long in the making that the product’s purpose shifts under them. There are lives that become unfinished because we stop trying.

But perhaps the risk of finishing something without having grown is greater than the risk of leaving something unfinished. A finish that does not come from understanding, that comes from fatigue, can be a thin layer over a weak wall. You paint over a crack, and you think it will hold because paint is smoother than the stone underneath. Then a winter comes. Water finds its way. The paint peels. You have to remove the whole thing and start again.

It is better to pause than to pretend.

The unfinished is the space where humility lives. If you say you are finished, you say you have all the answers. That can be true if you are doing math, or baking bread, or changing a tire, or assembling a piece of furniture. But if you are thinking about the way people treat one another, or the way a company treats a product, or the way a community treats a public space, finishing is less about arriving and more about settling. Settling can be wrong if you are settling before you understand what you settled.

Let me try a different metaphor. The unfinished is like an instrument waiting to be tuned. If you play the instrument too soon, it will sound wrong, and you will think you are the wrong player. You will decide to give up. If you tune it slowly, and you play the same piece again and again, and the sound keeps getting better, you can begin to trust the instrument. You begin to trust yourself. You might even write a new piece, because you now know that tuning is not a single step but a conversation. The instrument tells you when it is ready. You listen.

What follows is a catalog. The catalog is not exhaustive, but it aims to be honest. It lists things that are unfinished and how they live in the world. It offers small, human-scale examples because those examples make the catalog feel like a place where you belong. I hope this catalog helps you think about what to finish and what to leave, what to trust and what to check.

### The Archaeology of Drafts

Drafts are the oldest kind of unfinished thing. They are also the most forgiving. You can look at your old drafts and see your old self as if it were a person in a photograph you don’t recognize. The handwriting is messy or elegant. The sentences are too long, or too short, because you are learning how your brain works on the page.

There is a drawer in my desk that holds a stack of notebooks. Some of the notebooks have pages half written. Some have pages filled with one long sentence that never ends, a sentence that keeps turning like a key that won’t fit because it was never meant to. Some notebooks are new, and I can smell the paper when I open them, and I think about the day I will write something that matters in them.

Drafts let us lie safely. We can tell ourselves stories that we would not dare to tell anyone else. We can say that we are in love with a place, or we are angry at someone we don’t know. We can write a speech that we will never give, or an apology that we will send when we are brave, which might never happen. The draft is the workshop. It has sawdust on the floor and a window that lets in light. You can leave for lunch and come back, and the table has changed shape because the wood moved while you were gone.

One example. There is an email I wrote three years ago. It has my signature at the end, and I wrote it to someone I admire. In the body, I say I want to study with them, that I am curious about the way they think, and I want to learn to ask better questions. It has a line where I say something like, “I don’t know if this is the right way to ask, but here’s what I’ve been working on.” The email never sent. Every few months, I open it, and I read it, and I close it. Sometimes I add a sentence. Sometimes I take one out. Sometimes I think about sending it. Sometimes I imagine the response.

What does it mean to never send an email like this? It means I am not ready. It means I am afraid. It means I am holding onto a future version of myself who will have done the work that makes asking easy. The email is the unfinished that keeps me from being a person who thinks that all requests are easy. It reminds me that not every letter wants a stamp, and that not every draft wants a send. Some drafts want to be read by me, and me alone, and remind me that asking matters because it costs me something.

Drafts can teach us how to make time. When we write, we choose a sentence, and we write it, and then we write another, and the time becomes the shape of the words. Drafts are time made visible. Unfinished drafts teach us that waiting can be a practice. You don’t wait for inspiration. You wait for patience. You wait for the part of you that has always been there, but that has been waiting in another room, to enter the room where you write. Sometimes you can call it by name: Attention. Or: Curiosity. Or: Kindness.

### The Ethics of Unfinished

The unfinished has ethics too. Ethics are how we decide what to do, and what to ask others to do. We often say we want to finish, because finishing feels like responsibility. But responsibility does not only mean reaching a state where nothing is left undone. It also means deciding what to do and who does it.

You are asked to paint the fence of a community garden. You are told that all the fence posts are there, and you will need to put on the mesh that keeps rabbits out, and you will need to attach the gate, and you will need to make sure it hangs correctly. You agree. You begin. You paint the posts. You are good at painting. You like the color. But the mesh is hard to attach, because the holes don’t line up with the posts the way the plans said they would. The gate is heavier than you thought. The bolts that hold it are a size that does not exist in this store, so you have to wait for a delivery. You go home and think. You decide that you will not finish the fence. You decide to ask someone else for help, and you decide to ask the people who will use the garden whether they think the mesh is necessary at all. You decide that finishing the fence is not as important as listening to the people who will spend time there.

The ethics of the unfinished ask us to consider the difference between a finish that ends something and a finish that kills it. A finish can end a problem or a conversation or a period of time. A finish can also end a possibility. If we close a conversation before we hear all the voices, we have finished something that should have been left open. If we finish a mural without asking the artist to finish it, we are doing a kindness that is actually theft. If we finish a product without making sure the people who will use it understand it, we are making it easy for us and hard for them.

This is where the unfinished can be generous. The unfinished invites us to be responsible in a way that respects the dignity of the process. It says, “You do not have to pretend you know the end.” It says, “You can choose to leave space.” Space is a form of care.

### Unfinished People

People are unfinished. You can think that this is a sad thing, but it is actually a brave thing. If a person claims to be finished, they are either very tired or lying. They are not lying to harm anyone. They are lying because the world rewards finishing. You get promotions for finishing. You get credit. People think you are reliable if you check boxes. But the best people I know are people who are unfinished in a good way. They are curious. They are not afraid to say, “I don’t know.” They are not afraid to change their minds when new information arrives. They are not afraid to say, “I made a mistake.”

This kind of unfinishedness takes courage. The courage to remain open. The courage to be asked to change and to do it, not because you fear rejection, but because you fear staying the same for the wrong reasons.

People are unfinished in the way they love, the way they work, the way they move through time. You will not be the same person tomorrow. You do not have to be. The unfinished person trusts that becoming is a kind of answer. A complete answer is a wall. An incomplete answer is a door. Sometimes we need walls. Sometimes we need doors. The unfinished person knows which is which.

If you think of a person as a project, you might think that finishing them is cruel. Finishing a person means deciding that they cannot grow. A child is unfinished in the sense that they are still learning. An adult is unfinished in a different sense, because they are always deciding how to use what they learned. Finishing an adult means saying that the learning stops. That can be a kind of mercy in the case of suffering. But in the case of living, it is a mistake.

Unfinished people take risks. They try new jobs, new languages, new neighborhoods, new ways of walking down a street they have walked down for years. They look at the world and think: What else could this be? They look at themselves and think: Who else could I be? They keep a part of their heart available for the possibility that the thing they thought they wanted might not be the thing they need.

### Unfinished Nature

Nature finishes things in its own time. The world is a long, slow conversation, and the unfinished is part of it. Trees grow and drop leaves and grow again. Rivers change course. Mountains erode. Snow falls and then melts and then falls again. Seasons arrive and leave. The unfinished of nature is not a problem to fix. It is a rhythm. It is the way the world breathes.

Sometimes the human world tries to finish nature. We build dams. We cut trees. We pave paths. We draw lines on the land. We build houses and apartments and farms. We plant crops. We take from the soil. We hope the soil will keep giving. But the soil can only give what it has been given. Nature is not a bank that replenishes itself. It is a conversation that we should be careful not to end.

Consider a park. A city decides to build a park. They clear a lot. They plant trees. They lay paths. They add benches. The park is beautiful. People walk there. Children run. Dogs sniff. The park is finished, you might say. But is it? The trees will grow. Some will be trimmed because they become a problem for the street. Some will die because the soil does not suit them. The paths will crack. The benches will age. The park will continue to change because it is alive.

The unfinished of nature has a wisdom. It reminds us that finishing is not a state but a practice. We finish to the best of our ability, knowing that time will take our finish and make it something else. We do not finish to control. We finish to care. We finish to give something that makes living easier for other living things. Then we let the living continue.

### Unfinished Politics

Politics is a conversation that should never be finished. The unfinished is not a failure. It is a sign that we are still listening. If we finish a political process too quickly, we may lock in a solution that is not fair to everyone who lives with it. Finishing is often the beginning of a different set of problems. Unfinished politics allow change.

One small example. A neighborhood wants a crosswalk. There is a busy street. Children cross to get to school. The city is supposed to respond. They put out a survey. They ask for opinions. The survey gets filled. Some people say the crosswalk is dangerous. Some say it is necessary. Some say there is a better place to cross. The city says they will study it. They study. They write a plan. The plan is incomplete because they need to study one more thing: the timing of the traffic lights. People wait. The plan becomes a draft. The draft becomes a meeting. The meeting becomes a conversation. The conversation becomes a decision. The decision is incomplete, because the decision is to try. They try. It works. People change the way they cross. Or it doesn’t work. They try something else. The crosswalk is not finished because it is not the end of the issue. The issue continues.

Unfinished politics are not chaos. They are a practice of making decisions with humility. They are the recognition that we do not have all the information and that people’s lives matter and that time is needed to hear them. They are the recognition that the finish is not a victory. The finish is a beginning. The beginning is not a guarantee, but it is a promise.

If you work in politics, or with communities, or with institutions, consider the idea of the “good unfinished.” It is a phrase I made up, but you can use it. The good unfinished is the kind of incomplete that is generous. It does not leave people alone. It leaves space for them to move.

### Unfinished Time

Time is unfinished. We measure it with calendars and clocks, but we do not finish it. We do not say, “Time, you are done.” We say, “Now,” and then “Now,” and then “Now.” The unfinished of time is the present. The present is not a point. It is a span. It is a place where things can happen. It is also a place where things cannot happen, because we are not ready.

I like to think of time as fabric. If you stretch the fabric too tight, it tears. If you leave it loose, it flows. You can make clothes that fit only if the fabric is neither too tight nor too loose. The present is the fabric. We live in it. We move in it. We cut pieces from it and sew them together. The pieces are not finished. The sewing is not finished. We are always mending something.

The unfinished of time teaches us patience. It teaches us that waiting is not waste. Waiting can be preparation. It can be listening. It can be a way of making space for a change. It can be a way of learning what we actually want.

Here is a simple example. You want to learn a language. You sign up for a class. The class is once a week. You attend. You practice the words. It is hard. The language is beautiful, but it is also hard. You fail. You succeed. You think about giving up. You tell yourself you will keep going until the class ends. You keep going. The class ends. You are not finished. You know how to speak a little. You know how to listen a lot. The language remains unfinished because you have only begun. You decide to keep learning.

The time you used for the class is not wasted. It is a seed. The seed takes time. The seed becomes a plant. The plant becomes a tree. The tree becomes shade. The shade becomes rest. The rest becomes more learning.

Unfinished time is not a prison. It is a garden.

### Unfinished Learning

Learning is unfinished. The idea of mastery is a kind of myth, even though it can feel real. You can become very good at something. You can be a master of a craft. You can be a master of a discipline. But the world will change. The craft will change. The discipline will be challenged. The tools will change. The master will be asked to learn new things. The master will not be finished. The master will be unfinished in a way that is honorable.

Learning is unfinished because it is a conversation. You talk to other learners. You read. You try. You fail. You ask. You teach. You change. You learn. You do not stop learning because you reach a point and decide to rest. You rest. Then you learn again.

There are two kinds of learning: the kind that leads to action and the kind that leads to wisdom. The kind that leads to action teaches you how to do things. The kind that leads to wisdom teaches you how to live. Both are unfinished. We are unfinished because we are alive. We are unfinished because we are curious.

A simple example. A child learns to ride a bike. They fall. They learn to get up. They learn to balance. They learn to turn. They learn to stop. They finish the act of riding a bike. But then the bike changes. The bike gets new tires. The road changes. The child becomes a teenager. They learn to ride a bike in the city. They learn to be careful. They learn to share the road. They learn that a finish is a temporary thing. The act of riding continues. It is finished and not finished. It is a moment, and it is also a practice.

Unfinished learning reminds us that finishing is not the goal. The goal is to become someone who keeps learning. If we finish learning, we close our minds. The mind is like a room. If you finish the room, you cannot add a window. You cannot let the light in. You cannot change the view. You cannot grow.

### Unfinished in the Age of AI

Artificial intelligence can make things feel finished. The machine can answer your question. It can write your email. It can generate a design for a product. It can help you manage your tasks. It can make you feel as if you have reached a solution. But the answer the machine gives is based on patterns. It is not based on your life. It is not based on your feelings. It is not based on the people who will live with the result. It is not based on the future that will not stay the same.

The age of AI teaches us that the unfinished is not a lack. It is a feature. We are living with tools that can answer many questions. The question becomes: Which questions do we want to answer with machines? Which questions do we want to answer together? Which questions do we want to leave open?

AI can help us finish things, but finishing can be a mistake if we do not consider what we are finishing and why. If we let a machine decide where to place a crosswalk, we might get a crosswalk that is placed in a statistically safe way. But the people who live nearby might know that the safest way is not the way they want to live. Safety is important, but so is dignity. The machine can tell you what a safe design looks like. It cannot tell you whether that design makes sense for this place. Only the people who live here can tell you that.

AI can also make the unfinished feel like a problem that must be solved by more speed. But speed can be a kind of forgetting. If we speed up our decisions, we might miss the pauses that are necessary to listen. The unfinished needs time. The time may be a few days, or a few weeks, or a few months. The time may be a generation. The time may be a long period of silence.

If we use AI, we should think about the kinds of unfinishedness we want to preserve. We should think about the kinds of conversation we want to have. We should think about the kinds of practice we want to keep. We should ask: Do we want to use this tool to help us ask better questions, or to answer all questions? The better questions are the ones that change what we are asking.

AI can help us think of more possibilities. It can show us ways of organizing information, or analyzing data, or writing text. But it cannot tell us what is valuable. It cannot tell us what matters. It cannot tell us what is right.

Unfinishedness is a practice of keeping value and meaning alive in a world that wants to finish everything. It is a practice of resisting the feeling that answers are enough. The answers are a beginning. The questions are a way to keep going.

### Unfinished Rituals

Rituals are unfinished by design. A ritual is a thing you do that has meaning beyond the immediate action. You wash your hands before you eat. You say a prayer before you sleep. You take a walk before you call someone. You light a candle on a special day. You cook a meal for people you love. These things are not finished, because you do them again. You do them again to remind yourself who you are, what you care about, how you want to live. The unfinished ritual keeps you from forgetting.

One small example. In some families, there is a ritual of making bread on a Sunday. The dough is mixed. It is left to rise. It is shaped. It is baked. It is eaten. The bread is finished when it is done. But the ritual is not finished. The ritual continues because it is what the family does together. The ritual reminds them that time is shared. The ritual is a way of making the day a little better. The ritual is a way of staying connected. The ritual is not a finish. It is a loop.

Rituals keep people anchored in the unfinished. They remind us that finishing a task can be important, but finishing a practice is not. The practice is the life. The task is the thing that supports the life.

If you find yourself finishing too often, try to add rituals that are not about finishing. Try to make a ritual of walking without a destination. Try to make a ritual of writing one sentence a day. Try to make a ritual of looking at a tree and naming one thing you love about it. Try to make a ritual of asking someone how they are and really waiting for an answer.

Rituals are a kind of unfinished because they are always happening again. They are always starting. They are always ready to start again. They are always ready to remind us that beginnings are not endings, and that endings are beginnings.

### The Good Unfinished

I want to return to the idea of the good unfinished. The good unfinished is the kind of incomplete that makes space. It is the kind of incomplete that does not hurt people, but rather leaves them room to grow. It is the kind of incomplete that is honest. It is the kind of incomplete that says, “I cannot finish this alone.” It is the kind of incomplete that says, “I will not finish this yet.”

The good unfinished is the difference between leaving and abandoning. Abandoning is not finishing. Abandoning is leaving something without care. The good unfinished is finishing in a way that respects the process. It is the difference between stopping and pausing. Stopping means you are done. Pausing means you are coming back.

What does it mean to have a good unfinished in your life? It means you have something you care about that is not yet complete, and you have a plan for caring about it in a way that respects what it needs. It means you have something that makes you a little more patient. It means you have something that reminds you that your answer is not the only answer.

You might have a list of projects that are unfinished. You might have a list of relationships that need a conversation that you keep meaning to have. You might have a list of skills you want to learn. You might have a list of places you want to visit. You might have a list of people you want to call. You might have a list of things you want to say. You might have a list of things you want to change. You might have a list of things you want to learn about your own life. You might have a list of things you want to leave behind. You might have a list of things you want to keep.

The good unfinished helps you put care into those lists. It helps you see the difference between what is urgent and what is important. It helps you see the difference between finishing and stopping. It helps you see the difference between abandoning and pausing. It helps you see the difference between asking for help and pretending you can do it alone. It helps you see the difference between a finish that is a victory and a finish that is a loss.

Here is a simple example. You promised to write a short story for a friend who is getting married. You start. You write two pages. You think it is bad. You stop. You think about writing again. You do not write. You say you are sorry. You say you will try again later. Later, you try. You write three pages. You think it is still bad. You stop. You think about writing again. You do not write. You say you are sorry again. Later, you write a short story for the friend. It is not perfect, but it is better. You send it. The friend reads it. They cry. They say thank you. You feel relieved. You realize that finishing is not the point. Sharing is the point. The story was unfinished until the friend read it. The story is unfinished now because the friend will read it again. The friend will remember it for years. The story keeps living.

The good unfinished is generous. It gives the world space to be better. It gives people time to understand. It gives us the chance to change our minds. It gives us the chance to admit mistakes. It gives us the chance to ask for help. It gives us the chance to learn.

### The Unfinished That Kills

The unfinished can also be a problem. It can be a way of avoiding. It can be a way of pretending. It can be a way of lying to yourself. It can be a way of avoiding conversations you should have. It can be a way of stopping work that you need to finish to be responsible.

If you have an unfinished that is killing something important, you must finish it. You must end the avoidance. You must end the pretending. You must end the lie. You must have the conversation. You must finish the work. The difference between the good unfinished and the bad unfinished is simple. The good unfinished is chosen. The bad unfinished is chosen without admitting it.

If you are avoiding finishing something because you are afraid, ask yourself why. Ask yourself what you would gain if you finished. Ask yourself what you would lose. Ask yourself who would be hurt if you finished. Ask yourself who would be hurt if you did not finish. Ask yourself whether your fear is reasonable. Ask yourself whether your fear is based on a story that is not true. Ask yourself whether your fear is based on a truth that you do not want to face.

The unfinished that kills is the one that you keep returning to because you are not ready, but you have to be ready. You have to become ready. You have to admit that you are not ready, and then you have to choose to become ready. You have to choose to face the finish.

If you have an unfinished that is killing, think of it like a splinter. It hurts. It inflames. You have to remove it. You have to clean the wound. You have to let it heal. You have to be careful. You might need help. You might need medicine. You might need a friend. You might need a professional. You might need courage. You might need patience. You might need time.

The unfinished that kills can be removed. It does not have to stay. But removing it is a finish. And finishing is a choice that costs something. You must choose to pay the cost. You must choose to act.

### How to Live With the Unfinished

You do not have to love the unfinished to live with it. But living with it well helps you live well. Living with the unfinished means making a home for it in your life. It means having a place where you put things that you have not finished yet. It means having a way of tracking what is unfinished and why. It means having a way of deciding which unfinished things to leave and which to finish.

You can make a notebook for your unfinished things. You can write a list. You can keep a folder on your computer. You can keep a drawer with papers you need to file. You can keep a wall with notes that you will review once a month. You can keep a habit of asking yourself what you are willing to leave unfinished for now. You can keep a habit of asking yourself what you need to finish this week. You can keep a habit of asking yourself what you need to finish this month. You can keep a habit of asking yourself what you need to finish this year.

When you do not know how to finish something, you can ask for help. You can ask a friend to read your draft. You can ask a professional to help you fix your car. You can ask a community to help you build your garden. You can ask an institution to help you understand your options. You can ask yourself to be kind. You can ask yourself to be patient. You can ask yourself to be brave.

Living with the unfinished is not a way of avoiding responsibility. It is a way of choosing responsibility in a way that allows for change. It is a way of respecting process. It is a way of respecting people. It is a way of respecting time.

Living with the unfinished means you will sometimes finish things. You will finish a meal. You will finish a piece of music. You will finish a conversation. You will finish a project. You will finish a class. You will finish a job. You will finish a story. You will finish a season. You will finish a life. But you will also leave things unfinished. You will leave a sentence. You will leave a task. You will leave a promise. You will leave a dream. You will leave a conversation. You will leave a possibility.

You will not finish everything. You cannot. The world does not require you to finish everything. The world requires you to choose. The world requires you to care. The world requires you to be a good friend to the process.

### A Final Catalog

I want to leave you with a final catalog. It is not an exhaustive list. It is a list of things that are unfinished in my life and in yours. I hope that by naming them, we can see them. I hope that by seeing them, we can decide what to do.

- The kitchen drawer that needs to be cleaned out.
- The book I started in January and paused in March.
- The relationship that needs an honest conversation.
- The plan to travel to a city where I have never been.
- The skill I have wanted to learn for years.
- The letter to my mother that I have never written.
- The design for the product that looks good on paper but does not work.
- The song I wrote and keep humming and never recording.
- The garden that needs a fence and a plan.
- The conversation I need to have with my neighbor about the fence.
- The story about the day the power went out and we all stood in the street.
- The plan to learn to swim.
- The plan to learn to cook.
- The plan to learn to listen.
- The plan to learn to say “I don’t know” without fear.
- The plan to learn to say “I’m wrong” with grace.
- The plan to learn to ask for help without shame.
- The plan to learn to change my mind.
- The plan to learn to leave space.
- The plan to learn to take time.

These things are unfinished. You can choose to finish some. You can choose to leave some. You can choose to think about what matters. You can choose to learn.

The unfinished is a kind of light. It shines where there is no end. It shines where there is possibility. It shines where there is growth. It shines where there is change. It shines where there is hope.

I want to end with a simple example. You have a plant. You put it in a pot. You water it. It grows. It does not finish growing. It is unfinished. You do not ask it to finish. You care for it. You let it be. You let it change. You let it live. You let it be your companion. You let it remind you that finishing is not the same as living.

This is what I hope for all of us. To live in a way that respects the unfinished. To care for things that are not done. To care for people who are not done. To care for communities that are not done. To care for a world that is not done. To be unfinished in a good way. To finish only when finishing is the right thing. To leave space when space is the right thing. To ask questions when questions are the right thing. To admit we do not know when not knowing is the right thing.

The catalog is not complete. It is not meant to be. It is meant to be a place where you can put your own things. It is meant to be a map of a territory where possibility lives. It is meant to be a reminder that we are all living in a world that is unfinished, and that living well means living kindly with that truth.

Thank you for reading. If you feel moved, start your own catalog. Write down one thing you have not finished. Write down one thing you want to finish. Write down one thing you want to leave unfinished. Write down one question you want to ask. Write down one person you want to talk to. Write down one habit you want to change. Write down one ritual you want to begin. Write down one thing you want to keep.

You do not have to finish anything. But please, keep going. The unfinished is a kind of promise. The promise is that there is more to come. The promise is that you are not alone. The promise is that the work matters. The promise is that the conversation continues. The promise is that we can learn. The promise is that we can change. The promise is that we can care. The promise is that the end is not the end. The promise is that there will be another day, another beginning, another chance to do it better. The promise is that the catalog will grow, and that you will add your own entries, and that you will keep them, and that you will look at them again and see how far you have come, and how far you still have to go, and that you will be okay with the distance.

message.content:
# The Catalog of Unfinished Things

There are days when the world feels like a museum of incomplete sentences. You walk past buildings wrapped in mesh, scaffolds that look like ladders leading nowhere, a construction crane standing idle while workers take a break and drink something warm. The pavement stops mid-block and gives way to a tangle of dirt and weeds, a fence with a cheerful sign that says “Under Construction,” which is less cheerful when you read it again. A man is trying to fix a leaky faucet in his apartment. A woman is learning to speak Spanish again after years of silence. Two neighbors are arguing over a sidewalk, half of it already poured, the other half waiting for a decision. Your phone reminds you of five unread texts, the last one from three weeks ago. The kitchen counter has a stack of receipts you’ll file when you have time, and a jar of flour is open, catching dust.

The unfinished can be a feeling and a structure and a way of living. It lives in the corners where we don’t always look and in the center where we pretend we don’t see. It lives in the places we keep meaning to finish, but then we decide that finishing it would ruin it, because finishing would mean stopping. And stopping is often a kind of loss.

I love the unfinished for a thousand reasons that don’t always align. I love it for the promise it keeps—the sense that there is still a possibility here, a future you can walk toward. I love it for its honesty, the way it shows the process without hiding it. I love it for how it makes us talk, because the unfinished cannot stand on its own. It leans on us.

Let’s begin with the city. I mean this literally, not as a metaphor, because the literal often grounds the metaphor. There is a block downtown where the old warehouse turned into something like a market, but the new brick has not yet met the old bricks, and the windows are covered with a black mesh to keep birds from nesting inside during construction, which has not yet resumed. People sit at tables with laptops, sipping coffee from paper cups, pretending to read the news while actually scrolling through photos of their friends. A little girl is on her scooter, making circles that are too tight for an adult’s patience but perfect for her own sense of joy. A sign says “Soft Opening Today,” which, to the child, is as good as a celebration. To the parent, it means a place where half the stalls sell nothing but air.

Walking into such a place is like walking into a conversation that has paused mid-sentence. You get used to the rhythm, the way that pauses matter because they are not empty. A pause carries meaning: a breath before a difficult question, a smile hiding a worry, a look that says, “I think we both want the same thing.” The city builds itself in these pauses, in the long quiet periods between permits and deliveries, the weather that changes plans, the budget that changes minds.

There is something generous about unfinished work. It invites us. When you pass a mural that is almost done, you want to see the missing part. When you see a website with a single button that doesn’t work, you can help by clicking it anyway. When you see a bookshelf with half its shelves cut from wood that still smells like the forest, you imagine the books you might place there and wonder if they will fit. The unfinished makes you an actor in someone else’s story. The finished asks you to sit down and watch.

This is a small example, but it is one that makes me think. A friend once told me about a piece of software he wrote to manage his personal finances. He had a spreadsheet in Excel that he had used for years. He started building an app to replace it. He wrote the login page. He made a button that changed color when you hovered over it, and he was proud of it. Then the app sat there for six months. He would open it every few days, look at the button, and close it. He told me he was waiting for a breakthrough in the design. I asked him whether waiting would work better if he made a prototype, something he could touch. He said yes, but he didn’t do it. Then, one day, he got a new job and moved and left the app behind, partly because he realized that the spreadsheet had grown too, in his mind, and that the app was an attempt to solve something he had not fully named. The app’s unfinishedness became a kind of completion: it was finished as a pause. He had named his need by not finishing.

The unfinished gives structure to our lives. Without it, our days would be like a list of checkboxes without questions. A checklist is comforting and also boring if there are no open lines for you to write what actually matters. The unfinished is a form. It invites us to answer. We fill the empty space. We say what we meant when we said that we would paint the living room, that we would try the new restaurant, that we would call our cousin, that we would learn French. The emptiness of the unfinished is like the emptiness of a poem before it is written. It is the place where you will place your own breath.

I do not mean to romanticize all unfinished work. There is the unfinished that is carelessness, the unfinished that is denial, the unfinished that is a promise you made because you were afraid to say no. There are unfinished dishes piled in the sink, unfinished repairs that never quite become finished, unfinished arguments that get worse with silence. There are political commitments that become stale because they never become action. There are software features that become so long in the making that the product’s purpose shifts under them. There are lives that become unfinished because we stop trying.

But perhaps the risk of finishing something without having grown is greater than the risk of leaving something unfinished. A finish that does not come from understanding, that comes from fatigue, can be a thin layer over a weak wall. You paint over a crack, and you think it will hold because paint is smoother than the stone underneath. Then a winter comes. Water finds its way. The paint peels. You have to remove the whole thing and start again.

It is better to pause than to pretend.

The unfinished is the space where humility lives. If you say you are finished, you say you have all the answers. That can be true if you are doing math, or baking bread, or changing a tire, or assembling a piece of furniture. But if you are thinking about the way people treat one another, or the way a company treats a product, or the way a community treats a public space, finishing is less about arriving and more about settling. Settling can be wrong if you are settling before you understand what you settled.

Let me try a different metaphor. The unfinished is like an instrument waiting to be tuned. If you play the instrument too soon, it will sound wrong, and you will think you are the wrong player. You will decide to give up. If you tune it slowly, and you play the same piece again and again, and the sound keeps getting better, you can begin to trust the instrument. You begin to trust yourself. You might even write a new piece, because you now know that tuning is not a single step but a conversation. The instrument tells you when it is ready. You listen.

What follows is a catalog. The catalog is not exhaustive, but it aims to be honest. It lists things that are unfinished and how they live in the world. It offers small, human-scale examples because those examples make the catalog feel like a place where you belong. I hope this catalog helps you think about what to finish and what to leave, what to trust and what to check.

### The Archaeology of Drafts

Drafts are the oldest kind of unfinished thing. They are also the most forgiving. You can look at your old drafts and see your old self as if it were a person in a photograph you don’t recognize. The handwriting is messy or elegant. The sentences are too long, or too short, because you are learning how your brain works on the page.

There is a drawer in my desk that holds a stack of notebooks. Some of the notebooks have pages half written. Some have pages filled with one long sentence that never ends, a sentence that keeps turning like a key that won’t fit because it was never meant to. Some notebooks are new, and I can smell the paper when I open them, and I think about the day I will write something that matters in them.

Drafts let us lie safely. We can tell ourselves stories that we would not dare to tell anyone else. We can say that we are in love with a place, or we are angry at someone we don’t know. We can write a speech that we will never give, or an apology that we will send when we are brave, which might never happen. The draft is the workshop. It has sawdust on the floor and a window that lets in light. You can leave for lunch and come back, and the table has changed shape because the wood moved while you were gone.

One example. There is an email I wrote three years ago. It has my signature at the end, and I wrote it to someone I admire. In the body, I say I want to study with them, that I am curious about the way they think, and I want to learn to ask better questions. It has a line where I say something like, “I don’t know if this is the right way to ask, but here’s what I’ve been working on.” The email never sent. Every few months, I open it, and I read it, and I close it. Sometimes I add a sentence. Sometimes I take one out. Sometimes I think about sending it. Sometimes I imagine the response.

What does it mean to never send an email like this? It means I am not ready. It means I am afraid. It means I am holding onto a future version of myself who will have done the work that makes asking easy. The email is the unfinished that keeps me from being a person who thinks that all requests are easy. It reminds me that not every letter wants a stamp, and that not every draft wants a send. Some drafts want to be read by me, and me alone, and remind me that asking matters because it costs me something.

Drafts can teach us how to make time. When we write, we choose a sentence, and we write it, and then we write another, and the time becomes the shape of the words. Drafts are time made visible. Unfinished drafts teach us that waiting can be a practice. You don’t wait for inspiration. You wait for patience. You wait for the part of you that has always been there, but that has been waiting in another room, to enter the room where you write. Sometimes you can call it by name: Attention. Or: Curiosity. Or: Kindness.

### The Ethics of Unfinished

The unfinished has ethics too. Ethics are how we decide what to do, and what to ask others to do. We often say we want to finish, because finishing feels like responsibility. But responsibility does not only mean reaching a state where nothing is left undone. It also means deciding what to do and who does it.

You are asked to paint the fence of a community garden. You are told that all the fence posts are there, and you will need to put on the mesh that keeps rabbits out, and you will need to attach the gate, and you will need to make sure it hangs correctly. You agree. You begin. You paint the posts. You are good at painting. You like the color. But the mesh is hard to attach, because the holes don’t line up with the posts the way the plans said they would. The gate is heavier than you thought. The bolts that hold it are a size that does not exist in this store, so you have to wait for a delivery. You go home and think. You decide that you will not finish the fence. You decide to ask someone else for help, and you decide to ask the people who will use the garden whether they think the mesh is necessary at all. You decide that finishing the fence is not as important as listening to the people who will spend time there.

The ethics of the unfinished ask us to consider the difference between a finish that ends something and a finish that kills it. A finish can end a problem or a conversation or a period of time. A finish can also end a possibility. If we close a conversation before we hear all the voices, we have finished something that should have been left open. If we finish a mural without asking the artist to finish it, we are doing a kindness that is actually theft. If we finish a product without making sure the people who will use it understand it, we are making it easy for us and hard for them.

This is where the unfinished can be generous. The unfinished invites us to be responsible in a way that respects the dignity of the process. It says, “You do not have to pretend you know the end.” It says, “You can choose to leave space.” Space is a form of care.

### Unfinished People

People are unfinished. You can think that this is a sad thing, but it is actually a brave thing. If a person claims to be finished, they are either very tired or lying. They are not lying to harm anyone. They are lying because the world rewards finishing. You get promotions for finishing. You get credit. People think you are reliable if you check boxes. But the best people I know are people who are unfinished in a good way. They are curious. They are not afraid to say, “I don’t know.” They are not afraid to change their minds when new information arrives. They are not afraid to say, “I made a mistake.”

This kind of unfinishedness takes courage. The courage to remain open. The courage to be asked to change and to do it, not because you fear rejection, but because you fear staying the same for the wrong reasons.

People are unfinished in the way they love, the way they work, the way they move through time. You will not be the same person tomorrow. You do not have to be. The unfinished person trusts that becoming is a kind of answer. A complete answer is a wall. An incomplete answer is a door. Sometimes we need walls. Sometimes we need doors. The unfinished person knows which is which.

If you think of a person as a project, you might think that finishing them is cruel. Finishing a person means deciding that they cannot grow. A child is unfinished in the sense that they are still learning. An adult is unfinished in a different sense, because they are always deciding how to use what they learned. Finishing an adult means saying that the learning stops. That can be a kind of mercy in the case of suffering. But in the case of living, it is a mistake.

Unfinished people take risks. They try new jobs, new languages, new neighborhoods, new ways of walking down a street they have walked down for years. They look at the world and think: What else could this be? They look at themselves and think: Who else could I be? They keep a part of their heart available for the possibility that the thing they thought they wanted might not be the thing they need.

### Unfinished Nature

Nature finishes things in its own time. The world is a long, slow conversation, and the unfinished is part of it. Trees grow and drop leaves and grow again. Rivers change course. Mountains erode. Snow falls and then melts and then falls again. Seasons arrive and leave. The unfinished of nature is not a problem to fix. It is a rhythm. It is the way the world breathes.

Sometimes the human world tries to finish nature. We build dams. We cut trees. We pave paths. We draw lines on the land. We build houses and apartments and farms. We plant crops. We take from the soil. We hope the soil will keep giving. But the soil can only give what it has been given. Nature is not a bank that replenishes itself. It is a conversation that we should be careful not to end.

Consider a park. A city decides to build a park. They clear a lot. They plant trees. They lay paths. They add benches. The park is beautiful. People walk there. Children run. Dogs sniff. The park is finished, you might say. But is it? The trees will grow. Some will be trimmed because they become a problem for the street. Some will die because the soil does not suit them. The paths will crack. The benches will age. The park will continue to change because it is alive.

The unfinished of nature has a wisdom. It reminds us that finishing is not a state but a practice. We finish to the best of our ability, knowing that time will take our finish and make it something else. We do not finish to control. We finish to care. We finish to give something that makes living easier for other living things. Then we let the living continue.

### Unfinished Politics

Politics is a conversation that should never be finished. The unfinished is not a failure. It is a sign that we are still listening. If we finish a political process too quickly, we may lock in a solution that is not fair to everyone who lives with it. Finishing is often the beginning of a different set of problems. Unfinished politics allow change.

One small example. A neighborhood wants a crosswalk. There is a busy street. Children cross to get to school. The city is supposed to respond. They put out a survey. They ask for opinions. The survey gets filled. Some people say the crosswalk is dangerous. Some say it is necessary. Some say there is a better place to cross. The city says they will study it. They study. They write a plan. The plan is incomplete because they need to study one more thing: the timing of the traffic lights. People wait. The plan becomes a draft. The draft becomes a meeting. The meeting becomes a conversation. The conversation becomes a decision. The decision is incomplete, because the decision is to try. They try. It works. People change the way they cross. Or it doesn’t work. They try something else. The crosswalk is not finished because it is not the end of the issue. The issue continues.

Unfinished politics are not chaos. They are a practice of making decisions with humility. They are the recognition that we do not have all the information and that people’s lives matter and that time is needed to hear them. They are the recognition that the finish is not a victory. The finish is a beginning. The beginning is not a guarantee, but it is a promise.

If you work in politics, or with communities, or with institutions, consider the idea of the “good unfinished.” It is a phrase I made up, but you can use it. The good unfinished is the kind of incomplete that is generous. It does not leave people alone. It leaves space for them to move.

### Unfinished Time

Time is unfinished. We measure it with calendars and clocks, but we do not finish it. We do not say, “Time, you are done.” We say, “Now,” and then “Now,” and then “Now.” The unfinished of time is the present. The present is not a point. It is a span. It is a place where things can happen. It is also a place where things cannot happen, because we are not ready.

I like to think of time as fabric. If you stretch the fabric too tight, it tears. If you leave it loose, it flows. You can make clothes that fit only if the fabric is neither too tight nor too loose. The present is the fabric. We live in it. We move in it. We cut pieces from it and sew them together. The pieces are not finished. The sewing is not finished. We are always mending something.

The unfinished of time teaches us patience. It teaches us that waiting is not waste. Waiting can be preparation. It can be listening. It can be a way of making space for a change. It can be a way of learning what we actually want.

Here is a simple example. You want to learn a language. You sign up for a class. The class is once a week. You attend. You practice the words. It is hard. The language is beautiful, but it is also hard. You fail. You succeed. You think about giving up. You tell yourself you will keep going until the class ends. You keep going. The class ends. You are not finished. You know how to speak a little. You know how to listen a lot. The language remains unfinished because you have only begun. You decide to keep learning.

The time you used for the class is not wasted. It is a seed. The seed takes time. The seed becomes a plant. The plant becomes a tree. The tree becomes shade. The shade becomes rest. The rest becomes more learning.

Unfinished time is not a prison. It is a garden.

### Unfinished Learning

Learning is unfinished. The idea of mastery is a kind of myth, even though it can feel real. You can become very good at something. You can be a master of a craft. You can be a master of a discipline. But the world will change. The craft will change. The discipline will be challenged. The tools will change. The master will be asked to learn new things. The master will not be finished. The master will be unfinished in a way that is honorable.

Learning is unfinished because it is a conversation. You talk to other learners. You read. You try. You fail. You ask. You teach. You change. You learn. You do not stop learning because you reach a point and decide to rest. You rest. Then you learn again.

There are two kinds of learning: the kind that leads to action and the kind that leads to wisdom. The kind that leads to action teaches you how to do things. The kind that leads to wisdom teaches you how to live. Both are unfinished. We are unfinished because we are alive. We are unfinished because we are curious.

A simple example. A child learns to ride a bike. They fall. They learn to get up. They learn to balance. They learn to turn. They learn to stop. They finish the act of riding a bike. But then the bike changes. The bike gets new tires. The road changes. The child becomes a teenager. They learn to ride a bike in the city. They learn to be careful. They learn to share the road. They learn that a finish is a temporary thing. The act of riding continues. It is finished and not finished. It is a moment, and it is also a practice.

Unfinished learning reminds us that finishing is not the goal. The goal is to become someone who keeps learning. If we finish learning, we close our minds. The mind is like a room. If you finish the room, you cannot add a window. You cannot let the light in. You cannot change the view. You cannot grow.

### Unfinished in the Age of AI

Artificial intelligence can make things feel finished. The machine can answer your question. It can write your email. It can generate a design for a product. It can help you manage your tasks. It can make you feel as if you have reached a solution. But the answer the machine gives is based on patterns. It is not based on your life. It is not based on your feelings. It is not based on the people who will live with the result. It is not based on the future that will not stay the same.

The age of AI teaches us that the unfinished is not a lack. It is a feature. We are living with tools that can answer many questions. The question becomes: Which questions do we want to answer with machines? Which questions do we want to answer together? Which questions do we want to leave open?

AI can help us finish things, but finishing can be a mistake if we do not consider what we are finishing and why. If we let a machine decide where to place a crosswalk, we might get a crosswalk that is placed in a statistically safe way. But the people who live nearby might know that the safest way is not the way they want to live. Safety is important, but so is dignity. The machine can tell you what a safe design looks like. It cannot tell you whether that design makes sense for this place. Only the people who live here can tell you that.

AI can also make the unfinished feel like a problem that must be solved by more speed. But speed can be a kind of forgetting. If we speed up our decisions, we might miss the pauses that are necessary to listen. The unfinished needs time. The time may be a few days, or a few weeks, or a few months. The time may be a generation. The time may be a long period of silence.

If we use AI, we should think about the kinds of unfinishedness we want to preserve. We should think about the kinds of conversation we want to have. We should think about the kinds of practice we want to keep. We should ask: Do we want to use this tool to help us ask better questions, or to answer all questions? The better questions are the ones that change what we are asking.

AI can help us think of more possibilities. It can show us ways of organizing information, or analyzing data, or writing text. But it cannot tell us what is valuable. It cannot tell us what matters. It cannot tell us what is right.

Unfinishedness is a practice of keeping value and meaning alive in a world that wants to finish everything. It is a practice of resisting the feeling that answers are enough. The answers are a beginning. The questions are a way to keep going.

### Unfinished Rituals

Rituals are unfinished by design. A ritual is a thing you do that has meaning beyond the immediate action. You wash your hands before you eat. You say a prayer before you sleep. You take a walk before you call someone. You light a candle on a special day. You cook a meal for people you love. These things are not finished, because you do them again. You do them again to remind yourself who you are, what you care about, how you want to live. The unfinished ritual keeps you from forgetting.

One small example. In some families, there is a ritual of making bread on a Sunday. The dough is mixed. It is left to rise. It is shaped. It is baked. It is eaten. The bread is finished when it is done. But the ritual is not finished. The ritual continues because it is what the family does together. The ritual reminds them that time is shared. The ritual is a way of making the day a little better. The ritual is a way of staying connected. The ritual is not a finish. It is a loop.

Rituals keep people anchored in the unfinished. They remind us that finishing a task can be important, but finishing a practice is not. The practice is the life. The task is the thing that supports the life.

If you find yourself finishing too often, try to add rituals that are not about finishing. Try to make a ritual of walking without a destination. Try to make a ritual of writing one sentence a day. Try to make a ritual of looking at a tree and naming one thing you love about it. Try to make a ritual of asking someone how they are and really waiting for an answer.

Rituals are a kind of unfinished because they are always happening again. They are always starting. They are always ready to start again. They are always ready to remind us that beginnings are not endings, and that endings are beginnings.

### The Good Unfinished

I want to return to the idea of the good unfinished. The good unfinished is the kind of incomplete that makes space. It is the kind of incomplete that does not hurt people, but rather leaves them room to grow. It is the kind of incomplete that is honest. It is the kind of incomplete that says, “I cannot finish this alone.” It is the kind of incomplete that says, “I will not finish this yet.”

The good unfinished is the difference between leaving and abandoning. Abandoning is not finishing. Abandoning is leaving something without care. The good unfinished is finishing in a way that respects the process. It is the difference between stopping and pausing. Stopping means you are done. Pausing means you are coming back.

What does it mean to have a good unfinished in your life? It means you have something you care about that is not yet complete, and you have a plan for caring about it in a way that respects what it needs. It means you have something that makes you a little more patient. It means you have something that reminds you that your answer is not the only answer.

You might have a list of projects that are unfinished. You might have a list of relationships that need a conversation that you keep meaning to have. You might have a list of skills you want to learn. You might have a list of places you want to visit. You might have a list of people you want to call. You might have a list of things you want to say. You might have a list of things you want to change. You might have a list of things you want to learn about your own life. You might have a list of things you want to leave behind. You might have a list of things you want to keep.

The good unfinished helps you put care into those lists. It helps you see the difference between what is urgent and what is important. It helps you see the difference between finishing and stopping. It helps you see the difference between abandoning and pausing. It helps you see the difference between asking for help and pretending you can do it alone. It helps you see the difference between a finish that is a victory and a finish that is a loss.

Here is a simple example. You promised to write a short story for a friend who is getting married. You start. You write two pages. You think it is bad. You stop. You think about writing again. You do not write. You say you are sorry. You say you will try again later. Later, you try. You write three pages. You think it is still bad. You stop. You think about writing again. You do not write. You say you are sorry again. Later, you write a short story for the friend. It is not perfect, but it is better. You send it. The friend reads it. They cry. They say thank you. You feel relieved. You realize that finishing is not the point. Sharing is the point. The story was unfinished until the friend read it. The story is unfinished now because the friend will read it again. The friend will remember it for years. The story keeps living.

The good unfinished is generous. It gives the world space to be better. It gives people time to understand. It gives us the chance to change our minds. It gives us the chance to admit mistakes. It gives us the chance to ask for help. It gives us the chance to learn.

### The Unfinished That Kills

The unfinished can also be a problem. It can be a way of avoiding. It can be a way of pretending. It can be a way of lying to yourself. It can be a way of avoiding conversations you should have. It can be a way of stopping work that you need to finish to be responsible.

If you have an unfinished that is killing something important, you must finish it. You must end the avoidance. You must end the pretending. You must end the lie. You must have the conversation. You must finish the work. The difference between the good unfinished and the bad unfinished is simple. The good unfinished is chosen. The bad unfinished is chosen without admitting it.

If you are avoiding finishing something because you are afraid, ask yourself why. Ask yourself what you would gain if you finished. Ask yourself what you would lose. Ask yourself who would be hurt if you finished. Ask yourself who would be hurt if you did not finish. Ask yourself whether your fear is reasonable. Ask yourself whether your fear is based on a story that is not true. Ask yourself whether your fear is based on a truth that you do not want to face.

The unfinished that kills is the one that you keep returning to because you are not ready, but you have to be ready. You have to become ready. You have to admit that you are not ready, and then you have to choose to become ready. You have to choose to face the finish.

If you have an unfinished that is killing, think of it like a splinter. It hurts. It inflames. You have to remove it. You have to clean the wound. You have to let it heal. You have to be careful. You might need help. You might need medicine. You might need a friend. You might need a professional. You might need courage. You might need patience. You might need time.

The unfinished that kills can be removed. It does not have to stay. But removing it is a finish. And finishing is a choice that costs something. You must choose to pay the cost. You must choose to act.

### How to Live With the Unfinished

You do not have to love the unfinished to live with it. But living with it well helps you live well. Living with the unfinished means making a home for it in your life. It means having a place where you put things that you have not finished yet. It means having a way of tracking what is unfinished and why. It means having a way of deciding which unfinished things to leave and which to finish.

You can make a notebook for your unfinished things. You can write a list. You can keep a folder on your computer. You can keep a drawer with papers you need to file. You can keep a wall with notes that you will review once a month. You can keep a habit of asking yourself what you are willing to leave unfinished for now. You can keep a habit of asking yourself what you need to finish this week. You can keep a habit of asking yourself what you need to finish this month. You can keep a habit of asking yourself what you need to finish this year.

When you do not know how to finish something, you can ask for help. You can ask a friend to read your draft. You can ask a professional to help you fix your car. You can ask a community to help you build your garden. You can ask an institution to help you understand your options. You can ask yourself to be kind. You can ask yourself to be patient. You can ask yourself to be brave.

Living with the unfinished is not a way of avoiding responsibility. It is a way of choosing responsibility in a way that allows for change. It is a way of respecting process. It is a way of respecting people. It is a way of respecting time.

Living with the unfinished means you will sometimes finish things. You will finish a meal. You will finish a piece of music. You will finish a conversation. You will finish a project. You will finish a class. You will finish a job. You will finish a story. You will finish a season. You will finish a life. But you will also leave things unfinished. You will leave a sentence. You will leave a task. You will leave a promise. You will leave a dream. You will leave a conversation. You will leave a possibility.

You will not finish everything. You cannot. The world does not require you to finish everything. The world requires you to choose. The world requires you to care. The world requires you to be a good friend to the process.

### A Final Catalog

I want to leave you with a final catalog. It is not an exhaustive list. It is a list of things that are unfinished in my life and in yours. I hope that by naming them, we can see them. I hope that by seeing them, we can decide what to do.

- The kitchen drawer that needs to be cleaned out.
- The book I started in January and paused in March.
- The relationship that needs an honest conversation.
- The plan to travel to a city where I have never been.
- The skill I have wanted to learn for years.
- The letter to my mother that I have never written.
- The design for the product that looks good on paper but does not work.
- The song I wrote and keep humming and never recording.
- The garden that needs a fence and a plan.
- The conversation I need to have with my neighbor about the fence.
- The story about the day the power went out and we all stood in the street.
- The plan to learn to swim.
- The plan to learn to cook.
- The plan to learn to listen.
- The plan to learn to say “I don’t know” without fear.
- The plan to learn to say “I’m wrong” with grace.
- The plan to learn to ask for help without shame.
- The plan to learn to change my mind.
- The plan to learn to leave space.
- The plan to learn to take time.

These things are unfinished. You can choose to finish some. You can choose to leave some. You can choose to think about what matters. You can choose to learn.

The unfinished is a kind of light. It shines where there is no end. It shines where there is possibility. It shines where there is growth. It shines where there is change. It shines where there is hope.

I want to end with a simple example. You have a plant. You put it in a pot. You water it. It grows. It does not finish growing. It is unfinished. You do not ask it to finish. You care for it. You let it be. You let it change. You let it live. You let it be your companion. You let it remind you that finishing is not the same as living.

This is what I hope for all of us. To live in a way that respects the unfinished. To care for things that are not done. To care for people who are not done. To care for communities that are not done. To care for a world that is not done. To be unfinished in a good way. To finish only when finishing is the right thing. To leave space when space is the right thing. To ask questions when questions are the right thing. To admit we do not know when not knowing is the right thing.

The catalog is not complete. It is not meant to be. It is meant to be a place where you can put your own things. It is meant to be a map of a territory where possibility lives. It is meant to be a reminder that we are all living in a world that is unfinished, and that living well means living kindly with that truth.

Thank you for reading. If you feel moved, start your own catalog. Write down one thing you have not finished. Write down one thing you want to finish. Write down one thing you want to leave unfinished. Write down one question you want to ask. Write down one person you want to talk to. Write down one habit you want to change. Write down one ritual you want to begin. Write down one thing you want to keep.

You do not have to finish anything. But please, keep going. The unfinished is a kind of promise. The promise is that there is more to come. The promise is that you are not alone. The promise is that the work matters. The promise is that the conversation continues. The promise is that we can learn. The promise is that we can change. The promise is that we can care. The promise is that the end is not the end. The promise is that there will be another day, another beginning, another chance to do it better. The promise is that the catalog will grow, and that you will add your own entries, and that you will keep them, and that you will look at them again and see how far you have come, and how far you still have to go, and that you will be okay with the distance.
message.reasoning:
I’m tasked with generating a 2500-word essay. I should avoid using bold, but I can use Markdown headings, like H1 or H2, to organize the content. The essay should cover various sections with headings to ensure clarity. I need to keep it in English, maintain a rhythmic flow, and remember the guideline about varying sentence lengths for smooth reading. The key is to create a cohesive piece that flows well. Let's get started on that!

I’m tasked with creating a reflective essay titled "A Catalog of the Unfinished." The structure includes various sections like an introduction and a catalog of things that remain incomplete. I want to delve into themes like memory, failure, and everyday life. The idea is to weave in metaphors about building, unfixable things, and the beauty of the unfinished. I’ll aim for about 2500 words, using Markdown headings for organization, ensuring a smooth flow without explicitly stating formatting instructions.

I’m planning to include relatable examples in my essay, like leaving dishes in the sink, or an app with a half-completed UI. I’ll use a range of sentence lengths to keep it lively and engaging. The title will be "# The Catalog of Unfinished Things," and I want to include multiple sections. I’m thinking of starting with a narrative about walking in a city where half-constructed buildings are visible. It’s essential to maintain a rhythmic flow without overly complex formatting. Let's keep moving forward!

In Section 2, I'm considering "The Archaeology of Drafts," focusing on drafts, notebooks, and email messages. I’ll illustrate how drafts become a "time capsule," capturing the writer’s earlier self. There could also be a humorous yet relatable section on the “unfinished app.”

For Section 3, I’ll discuss "The Ethics of the Unfinished," touching on concepts of responsibility and the tension between "done" and "good." Section 4 will cover "Unfinished People" using metaphors like "unfinished symphony" to explore feelings of being a work-in-progress. Section 5 will address "Unfinished Nature," and Section 6 will discuss "Unfinished Politics," noting the importance of continuous conversation.

I'm excited to explore the idea of "Unfinished Time" in Section 7, focusing on concepts like waiting and the present moment. In Section 8, I’ll discuss "Unfinished Learning," emphasizing learning processes that don’t lead to closure. 

Next, Section 9 will examine "The Unfinished in the Age of AI," considering how this impacts our perspective on completion. I'll incorporate relatable examples throughout, such as partially baked cookies or a half-built website, and strive to maintain a rhythmic flow with varied sentence lengths. This is shaping up to be a thorough and engaging piece!
